build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors.

requirements.description: The player assigns crew members to station modules by dragging portraits, with each module requiring staffing to function and crew having skill ratings that affect performance. Score 0 if no crew assignment exists. Score 0.5 at most if crew can be assigned but skills have no effect. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

A power distribution system limits how many modules can be active simultaneously, and the player allocates power through a visible interface. Score 0 if no power system exists. Score 0.5 at most if power is mentioned but all modules always run regardless. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Random events fire periodically (supply ships, distress signals, system malfunctions, pirate attacks) requiring the player to respond via choice dialogs with visible consequences. Score 0 if no event system exists. Score 0.5 at most if events appear but choices have no consequence. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Crew fatigue accumulates with work and recovers with rest; overworked crew make mistakes that cause system failures. Score 0 if no fatigue system exists. Score 0.5 at most if fatigue is shown but has no gameplay effect. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Life support must be maintained or crew health degrades; game over occurs if life support fails completely or all crew are incapacitated. Score 0 if no failure condition exists. Score 0.5 at most if life support is shown but failure has no consequence. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

At least 6 distinct station modules (bridge, life support, power core, crew quarters, cargo bay, weapons, shields) with different functions and staffing needs. Score 0 if only one module exists. Score 0.5 at most if modules differ only in name with identical function. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least 8 distinct random event types with different requirements and consequences (trade offers, combat, repairs, rescues, discoveries). Score 0 if only one event type exists. Score 0.5 at most if events differ only in text with identical mechanics. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

New modules can be built using accumulated resources, expanding the station with at least 3 buildable additions beyond the starting layout. Score 0 if no building system exists. Score 0.5 at most if building is mentioned but no new modules can actually be added. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Event severity escalates over 30+ cycles, with early events being manageable and later events requiring full station optimization to survive. Score 0 if difficulty never increases. Score 0.5 at most if cycles pass but events remain equally easy. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A cycle or game-end summary shows cycles survived, events handled, crew status, modules built, and resources remaining. Score 0 if the game ends abruptly with no summary. Score 0.5 at most if only cycles survived is shown. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Normal startup shows a styled title screen with the game name, space station silhouette, and starfield imagery. Score 0 if the game starts directly in gameplay or uses raw default UI. Score 0.5 at most if a title exists but is sparse text on a blank background. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The flow connects title to station overview to crew management to event responses to cycle summaries without restarting the executable. Score 0 if major transitions are missing. Score 0.5 at most if transitions exist but break visually. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The station overview clearly shows module status, power allocation, crew assignments, and resource levels in readable non-overlapping regions. Score 0 if the player cannot determine station state. Score 0.5 at most if information is present but overlapping or unreadable. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Player actions produce visible feedback: crew assignment shows them moving to modules, power changes update module indicators, events show alert animations, and damage shows module degradation. Score 0 if actions happen silently. Score 0.5 at most if only one feedback type exists. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Art direction is coherent across title, station cross-section, modules, crew, events, and menus with a consistent sci-fi station palette and style. Score 0 if mostly default controls or mismatched placeholders dominate. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Station modules, crew portraits, event illustrations, resources, and equipment use real sprite art with distinct sci-fi designs. Score 0 if primary elements are unstyled rectangles. Score 0.5 at most if art exists but is inconsistent in style. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Animation brings the station to life: crew moving between modules, power fluctuation effects, event alert animations, module damage sparks, and starfield parallax. Score 0 if all elements are static. Score 0.5 at most if only one element animates. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

HUD and UI panels use styled themed widgets with sci-fi console aesthetics, holographic frames, or space-station styling rather than raw Godot defaults. Score 0 if all UI is default Label/Button. Score 0.5 at most if some panels are styled but others remain raw defaults. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
